proposed_revisions.rationale: All 10 partial_only solutions cover only asset.compliance and leave lifecycle_maintenance entirely uncovered, indicating that compliance_inspection should contain only the single capability asset.compliance. This suggests the current 2-cap compliance_inspection core is incorrectly bundling a compliance-only capability with a lifecycle or maintenance capability that vendors do not package together with inspection tools.

backing_evidence.reason: Covers asset.compliance only; no lifecycle_maintenance coverage demonstrates clean separation of concerns.
Safety-HSE solution covers compliance inspection exclusively; zero lifecycle ops coverage confirms distinct vendor positioning.
Hubble Safety Management covers only asset.compliance; the complete absence of lifecycle coverage validates the split.
SafetyCulture audit and compliance tool covers asset.compliance only; lifecycle_maintenance entirely absent in all partials.
Novade explicitly noted as covering 'only 1 of 9' capability IDs (asset.compliance); remaining 7 caps belong in separate core.

proposed_revisions.rationale: The consistent pattern of inspection/safety/e-PTW solutions covering only compliance indicates that inspection-centric vendors operate in a separate market segment from maintenance lifecycle vendors. Renaming and restructuring the cores to reflect inspection as its own domain (rather than bundled with lifecycle) better represents actual product positioning in the PSG portfolio.

backing_evidence.reason: E-Permit-To-Work solution is inspection/compliance-focused; no maintenance planning or execution coverage.
InSight ePTW & Safety System explicitly addresses compliance and safety audit; orthogonal to maintenance operations.
Integrated Worksite Monitoring covers inspection only; lifecycle_maintenance entirely uncovered across all partials.

proposed_revisions.rationale: The partial_only solutions consistently cover inventory master data (item_master, stock_levels, reorder_rules) and procurement basics (purchase_orders, vendor_master) as a natural operational bundle, while barcode_scanning and supply chain analytics emerge as a separate, less commonly implemented tier. Regrouping separates the transactional/operational core from the visibility/analytics layer, reflecting how vendors actually build and market these modules.

backing_evidence.reason: Covers all five transactional caps (item_master, stock_levels, reorder_rules, purchase_orders, vendor_master) as one cohesive block; lacks only barcode_scanning and analytics
Same pattern: five transactional/operational caps present; barcode_scanning and supply chain analytics are the cited gaps
Covers three inventory_operations caps plus partial supply chain analytics; absence of procurement modules (purchase_orders, vendor_master) is noted as a shortfall, not a design choice
Covers item_master, stock_levels, purchase_orders, vendor_master as the primary bundle; barcode_scanning and supply chain analytics are the missing components

proposed_revisions.rationale: An alternative split groups capabilities by functional domain: inventory execution (master data + stock visibility + barcode capture) versus procurement decision-making (reorder logic, purchasing, vendor management). This mirrors how mid-market ERP vendors often position inventory as a real-time operational module separate from procurement planning workflows, and explains why barcode_scanning appears frequently alongside stock_levels in partial solutions.

backing_evidence.reason: Covers barcode/QR scanning and stock visibility for order fulfillment; lacks procurement planning entirely, consistent with inventory_operations as a standalone operational tier
Clearly covers item_master and stock_levels; reorder_rules and procurement (purchase_orders, vendor_master) are absent, suggesting these belong to a separate procurement core
Covers item_master and stock_levels (inventory execution) plus purchase_orders and vendor_master (procurement), but split between two functional teams
Includes reorder_rules with procurement modules (purchase_orders, vendor_master), suggesting reorder logic is procurement-driven rather than inventory-only

proposed_revisions.rationale: The current split groups unrelated capabilities; vendors consistently package employee master/leave/attendance/self-service together as operational HR, payroll processing with all compensation/statutory reporting as a distinct payroll block, and recruitment/onboarding/performance as talent-focused capabilities. This realignment reduces the number of partial caps vendors must claim across core_hr_operations (4→4, but more cohesive) and consolidates the frequently-paired payroll-statutory stack (6 caps), leaving talent as a smaller, focused core.

backing_evidence.reason: Covers all three proposed cores with natural clustering: employee master/leave/attendance in hr operations, payroll/statutory in payroll_statutory, and performance in talent_performance.
Clearly separates leave/self-service (core_hr_operations) from the 5-cap payroll_statutory block, showing vendors build payroll+statutory as a single offering.
Demonstrates the natural vendor pattern: employee/leave/attendance bundled, payroll/statutory/compensation grouped, and performance isolated in talent.
Focuses solely on recruitment_ats and onboarding_offboarding, validating that talent/learning are packaged together separately from operations.
Specializes in time_attendance and leave_management with no payroll or talent, confirming these operational capabilities form a distinct cohesive block.

proposed_revisions.rationale: This revision maintains the existing payroll_compliance and talent_learning cores but reframes core_hr_operations as 'employee_master_operations' to emphasize that vendors see these four capabilities as a foundational operational cluster. Partial solutions consistently cover all four together (employee master, leave, attendance, self-service), never partial subsets, indicating they belong in one cohesive core rather than as isolated caps.

backing_evidence.reason: Covers all four operational caps (employee_master, leave, time_attendance implied) as a unified group, never showing gaps within the set.
Bundles employee_master, leave_management, and time_attendance as the operational backbone, with payroll as a separate concern.
Separates leave_management and self_service_portal (both in employee_master_operations) from the 5-cap payroll block, validating the operational grouping.
Shows employee_master, leave, and time_attendance always appear together in partial solutions, never fragmented across different vendor modules.
